name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ رَبُّكَ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ ۗ يَوْمَ يَأْتِى بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ لَا يَنفَعُ نَفْسًا إِيمَـٰنُهَا لَمْ تَكُنْ ءَامَنَتْ مِن قَبْلُ أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِىٓ إِيمَـٰنِهَا خَيْرًا ۗ قُلِ ٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ (١٥٨)
```

```english
Do they not consider that the angels may come to them, or your Lord may come, or some of your Lord’s signs may come? The day when some of your Lord’s signs do come, faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed beforehand and had not earned some goodness in its faith. Say, ‘Wait! We too are waiting!’ (158)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ شِيَعًا لَّسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ ۚ إِنَّمَآ أَمْرُهُمْ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (١٥٩)
```

```english
Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects, you will not have anything to do with them. Their matter rests only with Allah; then He will inform them concerning what they used to do. (159)
```

```arabic
مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا ۖ وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَىٰٓ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١٦٠)
```

```english
Whoever brings virtue shall receive [a reward] ten times its like; but whoever brings vice shall not be requited except with its like, and they will not be wronged. (160)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, and they contain a threat to those polytheists (المشركين) who disdained the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) and split into sects (شيعا), a clearance of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from those who divided their religion, a fair promise for whoever brings a righteous deed (الحسنة), and the fulfillment of the recompense.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ رَبُّكَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ** — "Do they await anything but that the angels should come to them, or your Lord should come, or some of your Lord’s signs should come?" (6:158). This is a rhetorical question of denunciation (استفهام إنكاري) in a situation where neither admonition benefits nor a call succeeds. Thus, the matters mentioned in the verse are inevitably things that are accompanied only by the judgment between them with justice and the decisive decree (الحكم الفصل) by their removal and the purification of the earth from their filth.

The necessity of this context is that the intended meaning of the coming of the angels is their descent with the sign of punishment, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ ، لَوْ ما تَأْتِينا بِالْمَلائِكَةِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ ، ما نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَما كانُوا إِذاً مُنْظَرِينَ** — "They say, ‘O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy! Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are truthful?’ We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they would not be granted any respite" (15:6-8).

The intended meaning of the coming of the Lord is the Day of Encounter (يوم اللقاء), which is the complete disclosure of the sign of monotheism (آية التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — such that no veil remains over it, as is the nature of the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) which is characterized by the removal of the covering. What justifies the application of "coming" (الإتيان) to that is the manifestation after concealment and presence after absence; exalted is His majesty (جل شأنه) above being described with the attributes of bodies.

It is sometimes said that the intended meaning is the coming of the command of the Lord, and its likeness has already passed in His saying, the Exalted: **هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِنَ الْغَمامِ** — "Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them in the shades of the clouds" (2:210), in the second volume of this book.

The intended meaning of the coming of some of the signs of the Lord is the coming of a sign that necessitates the transformation of the realm of life (نشأة الحياة) for them, such that there is no way to return to the expanse of choice (فسحة الاختيار). This is like the sign of death, which transforms the realm of action (نشأة العمل) into the realm of the intermediate recompense (جزاء البرزخي) — *the state between death and resurrection* — or it necessitates the settling of the disposition (ملكة) of disbelief and denial in their souls with a permanence that makes it impossible for them to submit to monotheism (التوحيد) and turn their hearts toward the truth, except for what might be on their tongues out of fear of the encompassing wrath and punishment. This is perhaps indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ أَنَّ النَّاسَ كانُوا بِآياتِنا لا يُوقِنُونَ** — "When the word falls against them, We shall bring out for them a monster from the earth who shall speak to them that the people had no faith in Our signs" (27:82).

Likewise is His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْفَتْحُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ ، قُلْ يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ لا يَنْفَعُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِيمانُهُمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ** — "They say, ‘When will this judgment be, if you are truthful?’ Say, ‘On the day of judgment their faith will not benefit the faithless, nor will they be granted any respite’" (32:29-30). For it is apparent that the intended meaning of "the judgment" (الفتح) is the victory for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by the decree between him and his community with justice, as Allah the Exalted narrated from Shu‘ayb (عليه‌السلام) in his saying: **رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْفاتِحِينَ** — "Our Lord! Judge between us and our people with justice, and You are the best of judges!" (7:89), and as He narrated from His messengers in His saying: **وَاسْتَفْتَحُوا وَخابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ** — "They sought victory, and every obstinate tyrant was disappointed" (14:15).

Or it necessitates a might (بأسا) from Allah the Exalted that cannot be turned back or escaped, so Allah compels them to faith so they may protect themselves from the painful punishment, but that does not benefit them. For faith (الإيمان) does not benefit except what was out of choice, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا قالُوا آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَحْدَهُ وَكَفَرْنا بِما كُنَّا بِهِ مُشْرِكِينَ ، فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمانُهمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا سُنَّتَ اللهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبادِهِ وَخَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْكافِرُونَ** — "Then, when they sighted Our punishment, they said, ‘We believe in Allah alone, and we disbelieve in what we used to ascribe as partners to Him.’ But their faith did not benefit them when they sighted Our punishment—Allah’s precedent that has passed among His servants—and there the faithless were losers" (40:84-85).

So these things—namely the coming of the angels, or the coming of the Lord, or the coming of some of His signs—are matters that accompany the decree between them with justice. Since no proof (حجة) affects them and no admonition (موعظة) benefits them, they await nothing but that, even if they are heedless of it; for what lies before them is a reality, whether they know it or ignore it.

It is sometimes said that the interrogation is for mockery (التهكم), as they used to propose to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he should bring down angels upon them, or that they should see their Lord, or that he should bring them a sign as the former ones were sent. So it is as if it were said: These people do not want a proof; they are only waiting for the things they proposed.

This view is not far-fetched regarding the beginning of the verse, but its end—namely His saying: **يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ** (the day when some of your Lord’s signs come)—does not suit it with such harmony, for mockery does not usually extend into the explanation of realities and the detailing of effects.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ** to the end of the verse, explains the specific characteristic of the day of the appearance of these signs. In reality, it is a characteristic of the signs themselves: that faith (الإيمان) does not benefit a soul that did not believe before that day with a faith of obedience and choice, or believed before it but had not earned any good in its faith and did not perform righteous deeds (صالحا), but rather was immersed in evil deeds and acts of disobedience. This is because there is no repentance (توبة) for such a person. The Exalted said: **وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ** — "But repentance is not for those who go on committing misdeeds until, when death visits one of them, he says, ‘I repent now’" (4:18). Thus, the soul that did not believe previously with a faith of obedience and pleasure, or believed in Allah but denied the signs of Allah and paid no heed to any of the laws of Allah, and indulged in destructive sins and earned nothing of righteous work (صالح العمل) when it was required of it, then witnessed the divine might and was driven by necessity to faith to repel the might of Allah the Exalted—that did not benefit it, nor did it repel any might from it; and His might is not repelled from the guilty people.

In the verse, there is a marvelous composition and subtle context in that He repeated the word "your Lord" (رَبُّكَ) three times. This is only to support the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) against his adversaries, the polytheists (المشركون), as they used to boast of their lords and take pride in their idols. This was so he might find strength in his Lord, his heart might be firm through Him, and his courage might be bolstered in his call—if it succeeds, otherwise by the decisive decree (القضاء الفصل) with which his Lord judges between him and his adversary. Then He emphasized that and increased the reassurance of his soul by saying at the conclusion of the verse: **قُلِ انْتَظِرُوا إِنَّا مُنْتَظِرُونَ** — "Say, ‘Wait! We too are waiting’" (6:158). That is: wait for what they are waiting for, and inform them that you are in expectation of it, and command them to wait for it, for it is the decisive matter and not a jest.

From here it becomes clear that the verse contains a serious threat, not a formal scaring. By this, the invalidity of what some mentioned in refuting the one who said the interrogation in the verse is for mockery becomes apparent. He said: "These three signs are what they, like others, await in reality, so it is not correct that this 'some' refers to anything they proposed; because the granting of signs proposed to the messengers necessitates, in the sunnah of Allah, the destruction of the community by an exterminating punishment if they do not believe in it, and Allah does not destroy the community of the Prophet of Mercy." [End of quote].

The flaw in this is that the indication of the Quranic verses that this community will be encompassed by the decree between them with justice and the decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل) is something that cannot be hidden, such as His saying: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ ، وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ ، قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ** — until He said — **وَيَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ قُلْ إِي وَرَبِّي إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ** — "Every nation has an apostle. So when their apostle comes, judgement is passed between them with justice, and they are not wronged. They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ Say, ‘I have no power to bring myself any benefit or harm, except what Allah may wish. There is a time for every nation... They ask you, ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord! It is indeed true, and you cannot frustrate [Him]’" (10:47-53).

The verse has been used as evidence that faith (الإيمان) has no effect if it is not coupled with action, and this is true in general (في الجملة), not absolutely. For the verse is in the position of explaining that whoever had it in his power to believe in Allah but did not believe, or had it in his power to believe and perform righteous deeds but believed and did not perform righteous deeds until the severe divine might overtook him and compelled him to that, then he does not benefit from his faith. As for the one who believed willingly but death overtook him and his term did not grant him respite to perform righteous deeds and earn good in his faith, the verse does not address his condition. Rather, the verse is not devoid of an intimation or indication that what is beneficial is only faith when it is out of willingness and has not been encompassed by sin nor corrupted by evil deeds.

**In His saying:** **لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها لَمْ تَكُنْ آمَنَتْ** — "its faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed" (6:158), there is a separation between the described (الموصوف) and the description (الوصف) by the subject of the verb, which is "its faith" (إيمانها). It seems this is to avoid a long separation between the verb and its subject, and the convergence of "in its faith" (فِي إِيمانِها) and "its faith" (إيمانُها) in wording.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً لَسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ** — "Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects, you have nothing to do with them" (6:159) etc. Although the preceding discourse was with the polytheists (المشركين) who were afflicted with splitting the primordial religion (الدين الحنيف), and the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) also had a share of the discourse and were perhaps alluded to, and the necessity of that is that His saying: "those who split up their religion and became sects" applies to the polytheists, rather to them and to the Jews and Christians due to the commonality of all in splitting and differing in the divine religion.

However, the connection of the discourse to the verses explaining the general divine laws—which begin with the prohibition of polytheism (الشرك) and end with the prohibition of splitting from the way of Allah—requires that His saying: "those who split up their religion and became sects" be a subject for explaining the state of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with whoever possesses this description. Thus, the use of the past tense in His saying: "split up their religion" (فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ) is to explain the principle of occurrence, whether in the past, present, or future, not the occurrence of the act in the past time only.

It is well known that the distinction of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his exclusion from those who differ in religion and are split into sects—each sect following a leader who guides them—is only because he is a messenger calling to the word of truth and the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد). He is a perfect example who represents Islam by his existence and calls to it by his action. Thus, the meaning of His saying: "you have nothing to do with them" (لَسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ) returns to the fact that they are not upon your religion to which you call, nor upon the level of your path which you tread.

So the meaning of the verse is that those who split their religion through differences that inevitably arise from knowledge (العلم)—"and those who were given it did not differ except out of envy among themselves"—and through sectarian divisions, are not upon your path which is built upon the unity of the word and the negation of division. Their matter in this splitting is only with their Lord; nothing of them adheres to you. Then He will inform them on the Day of Resurrection of what they used to do and disclose to them the reality of their deeds for which they are held hostage.

It has become clear from what preceded that there is no reason to restrict the verse to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) clearance from the polytheists, or from them and the Jews and Christians, or from those who differ in schools of thought (المذاهب) and innovations (البدع) from this community. Rather, the verse is general and encompasses everyone.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها وَمَنْ جاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَلا يُجْزى إِلَّا مِثْلَها وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ** — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like, and whoever brings an evil deed shall be requited only with its like, and they shall not be wronged" (6:160). The verse is complete in itself, disclosing a divine favor (منة إلهية) that He bestows upon His servants: that He rewards a righteous deed (الحسنة) with ten times its like, and does not reward an evil deed (السيئة) except with its like. That is, He counts the righteous deed as ten and the evil deed as one, and He is not unjust in the fulfillment, so He does not decrease from the former nor increase in the latter. If it is possible that He increases the reward of the righteous deed, He increases it beyond ten, as indicated by His saying: **مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنْبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنابِلَ فِي كُلِّ سُنْبُلَةٍ مِائَةُ حَبَّةٍ وَاللهُ يُضاعِفُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** — "The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain which grows seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains. Allah enhances severalfold whomever He wishes" (2:261). And it is possible that He pardons the evil deed, so He does not count the right of the single like.

However, by its connection to what preceded it and its arrangement with it in a single context, it yields another meaning. It is as if it were said after the discourse in the previous verses regarding agreement and gathering upon the truth and splitting therein: These are two traits, a righteous one and an evil one, in which what is similar to them is rewarded, and there is no injustice, for the reward is similar to the deed. So whoever brings the righteous deed, for him is its like and it is multiplied for him; and whoever brings the evil deed—which is the prohibited division—he shall not be rewarded except with an evil deed like it, and let him not covet a good reward. The meaning returns to something similar to what was understood from His saying: **وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها** — "The recompense of an evil deed is an evil deed like it" (42:40), that the intended meaning is to explain the similarity of the reward of the evil deed to it in being an evil that is not desired, not to establish unity and negate multiplication.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them) regarding His saying: **يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها** — "the day when some of your Lord’s signs come, its faith shall not benefit any soul" (6:158). He said: "The rising of the sun from the west, the emergence of the Beast (الدابة) and the Smoke (الدخان). And the man who was persistent [in sin] and did not perform the work of faith—then the signs come, so his faith does not benefit him."

**I say:** His saying: "the man who was persistent..." etc., is an explanation of His saying: **أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً** — "or earned some good in its faith" (6:158) according to what we presented, and it is indicated by the following narration.

In the same source, from Abu Basir from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them) regarding His saying: **أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً** — "or earned some good in its faith" (6:158). He said: "The sinning believer (المؤمن العاصي) whose many sins and few righteous deeds stood between him and his faith—so he did not earn any good in his faith."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, my father narrated to me from Safwan from Ibn Muskan from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ** — "the day when some of your Lord’s signs come" (6:158). He said: "When the sun rises from its west—then everyone who believes on that day, his faith will not benefit him."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and ‘Abd ibn Humayd in his *Musnad*, al-Tirmidhi, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: **يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ** — "the day when some of your Lord’s signs come" (6:158). He said: "The rising of the sun from its west."

**I say:** It is apparent that the narration is of the nature of "application" (الجري), and likewise the preceding narrations, or it could be from the commentary (التفسير). In any case, it is a day in which the divine seizure (البطشة الإلهية) appears, which compels people to faith, and it does not benefit them. The rising of the sun from its west has been mentioned in very many traditions from the paths of the Shi‘a from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) and from the paths of the Sunnis from a group of the companions such as Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, Ibn Mas‘ud, Abu Hurayrah, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, Hudhayfah, Abu Dharr, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas, ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa, Safwan ibn ‘Assal, Anas, ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn ‘Awf, Mu‘awiyah, Abu Umamah, ‘A’ishah, and others, even if they differ in their contents significantly.

Scientific views today do not prevent the change of the earth's motion contrary to what it is today of eastern motion, or the change of the two poles by the northern becoming southern and vice versa, either gradually as explained by astronomical observations or suddenly due to a total atmospheric event. All of this is if the word is not a symbol (رمز) pointing to a secret among the secrets of realities.

Among those signs, the narrations have counted the emergence of the Beast of the Earth (دابة الأرض), the Smoke (الدخان), and the emergence of Gog and Magog (يأجوج ومأجوج), and these are matters spoken of by the Noble Quran. They also counted other things such as the emergence of the Mahdi (peace be upon him), the descent of Jesus son of Mary, the emergence of the Dajjal, and others. Although these are from the events of the end of times, it is not clear that they are among those by which the door of repentance (باب التوبة) is closed.

In *al-Burhan*, from al-Barqi with his chain from ‘Abdullah ibn Sulayman al-‘Amiri from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him). He said: "The earth has never been except that Allah has a proof (حجة) in it—through whom the lawful and the prohibited are known, and who calls to the way of Allah. The proof is never cut off from the earth—except for forty days before the Day of Resurrection. So when the proof is raised and the door of repentance is closed—its faith shall not benefit any soul—that had not believed before the proof was raised. Those are the worst of Allah's creation, and they are the ones upon whom the Resurrection will occur."

**I say:** It was also narrated by Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari in the book *Manaqib Fatima* with another chain from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, from his father from al-Nadr from al-Halabi from Mu‘alla ibn Khunays from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً** — "Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects" (6:159). He said: "By Allah, the people departed from their religion."

**I say:** Meaning by the difference of schools of thought (المذاهب). The tradition regarding the community splitting into seventy-three sects has already passed.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse. He said: "Ali (peace be upon him) used to recite it: 'they departed from their religion' (فارقوا دينهم)."

**I say:** This recitation is narrated from him (peace be upon him) from some Sunni paths as well, as in *al-Durr al-Manthur* and others.

In *al-Burhan*, from al-Barqi from his father from al-Nadr from Yahya al-Halabi from Ibn Muskan from Zurarah. He said: "Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked while I was sitting—about the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: **مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها** — 'Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like' (6:160). Does it apply to those who do not recognize this matter [the Imamate]?" He said: "It is only for the believers (المؤمنين) specifically." I said to him: "May Allah set you right, what do you say of one who fasts and prays—and avoids the prohibited and has good piety (ورع) among those who do not recognize [the Imamate] nor show enmity?" He said: "Indeed, Allah will enter those into Paradise by His mercy."

**I say:** The narration indicates that the reward is in proportion to the recognition (المعرفة). Regarding this meaning, there are narrations reported from the paths of both groups.

There are many narrations regarding the meaning of His saying: **مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها** — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like" (6:160) etc., narrated by both groups and cited in the commentary of the verse. However, they are reported regarding the identification of instances (المصاديق) such as fasting, prayer, and others; we have omitted their citation for that reason.
